text: We report a case of a fourteen year old male with a history of ulcerative colitis, presents with severe abdominal pain and anorexia. His past surgical history is significant for complicated three-stage pouch rectal anastomosis. Patient was diagnosed with pouch dysfunction and long rectal cuff during exam under anesthesia.

During the transabdominal redo pouch surgery, the surgeon identifies and dissects severe adhesions. The remnant mesorectum surrounding the pouch is exposed, and the left superior rectal artery is carefully dissected and taken down.
The surgeon continues rectal dissection for completion proctectomy, revealing the anterior nonviliar's fascia.
In reoperative pouch surgery during rectal dissection, it is important to not to chase minor bladings.

The surgeon mobilizes the ileal pouch anteriorly and laterally, using a linear stapler to dissect the ileal pouch rectal anastomosis. The pouch is then separated from the remnant rectum and prepared for a new ileal pouch anal anastomosis.
The surgeon secures a 28mm circular EEA stapler anvil within the ileal pouch and establishes the reanastomosis. A leak test is performed, showing no leakage from the anastomosis site.
A new loop ileostomy is created, and the remnant rectum is removed and sent for pathological examination.
